data.title: BB Seguridade Q2: Sustained Pressure Slows Near-Term Growth
data.description:
I am downgrading BB Seguridade (OTCPK:BBSEY) from Hold to Sell after the company reported its 2Q25 results on August 5.

Recurring net income came in strong in the most recent quarter, up nearly 20% year-over-year and 12% quarter-over-quarter, validating the company's efficiency despite some persistent operational hiccups. The bigger problem here was the downward revision of guidance, especially at Brasilseg and Brasilprev, which limits enthusiasm and suggests a very weak H2 ahead, in which I believe it may continue the underperformance trend seen in H1.

Although in the long term the thesis remains compelling, with relatively attractive dividend yields (considering country risk), my bearishness is based on a six-month outlook. Given valuations don't look particularly attractive, I have my doubts as to whether there's any margin of safety sufficient to cover a more conservative outlook ahead, considering a scenario of weaker growth at Banco do Brasil (OTCPK:BDORY) (parent company), which tends to impact Brasilseg's brokerage revenues and premiums earned.

Recapping the Thesis

BB Seguridade has five core segments, with the following characteristics:

  • Brasilseg: very high relevance in the P&L, with medium risk and growth levers in Banco do Brasil credit and agribusiness.
  • Brasilprev: high relevance in the P&L, with low risk and interest rates and funding as growth levers.
  • BB Broker: also highly relevant to the P&L, but with low risk, with Banco do Brasil sales as the main driver.
  • Brasilcap and Brasildental: less relevant segments, with medium risk (more volatile results) and low risk (depending on base growth), respectively.

In May, right after the company reported its 1Q25 results, I wrote the following concerning BB Seguridade:

Despite the recent hiccups experienced in 1Q25 for BB Seguridade due to weak volume growth numbers—indexing potential demand weakness—profitability remains solid due to growth in net investment income, as a consequence of high interest rates in the country. And consequently, stable guidance for 2025 provides good stability for the dividend thesis, which remains above 9% for 2025."

Among these hiccups, the weaker performance of its most representative division, Brasilseg (insurance unit), stood out, where it saw weakness in sequential purchase down by (12.4%) in adjusted income, a consequence of the drop in premium writing, which fell in 1Q24 (6.5%) QoQ and (5.9%) YoY.

Thus, based on Brasilseg's guidance for premiums written for 2025, the (5.9%) reported in 1Q25 fell well short of the 2% to 7% initially projected. What saved the day was non-interest operating results growing by 4.2% in 1Q25, still within the initial projections of 3% to 8%—but largely due to the robust contribution of the financial results of Brasilseg and Brasilprev, which are still benefiting from the high interest rate environment (Selic currently at 15%), which favors the performance of floating-rate portfolios.

On BB Seguridade's 2Q25 Earnings

In 2Q25, looking at the full picture, the result is not bad.

Recurring net income was R$2.24 billion, up 12.2% QoQ and 19.7% YoY, in a performance driven by strong financial results (once again benefiting from the high interest rate scenario) and an improvement in claims in the insurance segment. Another positive point is that all four core segments (Brasilseg, Brasilprev, Brasilcap, and BB Broker) saw sequential profit growth, something that had not been seen since 2023.

Brasilseg was the main highlight, with a 25.5% YoY and 17.1% QoQ increase in profitability against the backdrop of high interest rates, and also positively impacted by a non-recurring effect of R$129 million, resulting from the reversal of legal provisions after a legislative change. It also performed well in terms of claims, with declines of (4.8 pp QoQ) and (8.8 pp YoY), helping to strengthen the bottom line.

Brasilprev reported net income jumping 17% QoQ and 19.8% YoY, also driven by financial results and a favorable effect of IGP-M (Brazil's General Market Price Index) deflation on the cost of liabilities—as a significant portion of Brasilprev's liabilities is linked to this index, reducing the monetary correction of obligations.

But when projecting what lies ahead, the scenario points to significant structural challenges. The same hiccups from 1Q25 not only persisted but worsened. Difficulties in expanding insurance premiums and continuing to record negative net inflows in pension plans forced a downward revision in guidance for 2025.

On the operational side, Brasilseg's premiums written fell (7.6%) QoQ and (0.5%) YoY, with pressure mainly in the life and agricultural segments, with a quarterly decline of (11%) and an annual decline of (22.9%), reflecting a scenario of contraction in rural credit from Banco do Brasil—a consequence of climatic events, very high interest rates, and increased delinquency.

For Brasilprev, gross pension contributions fell (27%) QoQ and (21.6%) YoY, pressured by reduced funding after the federal decree that began taxing IOF (Brazilian financial transaction tax) on pension plans for monthly contributions above R$50,000 from the end of May—although this was later revoked to R$300,000.

Even with figures generally in line with consensus in 2Q25, these red flags mentioned above in operational terms led BB Seguridade to revise its annual guidance downward.

The expected growth in written premiums went from 2% to 7% to (4%) to 1%, operating income was adjusted from 3% to 8% to 1% to 4%, and pension plan reserve growth fell from 12% to 16% to 9% to 12%. Ouch!

On Dividends

With consensus pointing to revenues of $1.81 billion (approximately R$9.95 billion) and considering a net income margin of 84.5%, net income would be $1.53 billion or R$8.4 billion. My past projections anticipated a net income of ~R$9 billion for this year.

If BB Seguridade's payout continues to range between ~80% and 90%, as hinted by the management team, it is still possible to project, at the lower end, US$1.2 billion in dividends, which at the current share price of $6.14, and assuming no changes in shares outstanding, the yield would be ~10-11%—higher than my previous projections of ~9%-10%. It is important to note that this is due to the decline in the ADR price from $6.54 to $6.20 since I made my initial projections.

I do not see this as an advantage in this case because the higher yield does not come from operational improvements but rather from the share price decline.

The slower growth, confirmed by more conservative guidance, shows that, in my view, the market may be right to punish BBSEY a little.

I also reiterate the issue of country risk. BBSEY's dividends come from an emerging asset exposed to fiscal risks, inflation, and exchange rate volatility. The 10Y U.S. Treasury has yielded ~4.3%, and real interest rates in Brazil (Selic minus inflation) are 5% per year, well above those in the U.S. Furthermore, even if BBSEY pays 10% in dividends in USD today, any devaluation of the BRL can erode the real return, as the company's profits are in BRL and converted to USD.

For example, as we saw in 2024, the BRL fell more than 20% against the USD, and in that scenario, much of the 8.5% dividend yield in 2024 was arguably wiped out.

On Valuations

Updating the Residual Income Model (RIM), based on an increase in ROE from 1Q25 to 2Q25 from 74% to 79% (driven by a sequential increase in stockholders equity of 30% reflecting strong net income), considering the same book value per share (BVPS) of $1.05, cost of capital (Ke) of ~16% (Selic rate + 0.35 beta + 5% ERP), and long-term growth rate (g) of 3%, the implied price for BBSEY is $6.14, when the ADR currently trades at $6.20.

However, I believe that, given the weaker guidance and structural headwinds in Banco do Brasil's credit scenario, which are affecting BB Seguridade's written premiums, the impact may still be even more pessimistic going forward, implying no clear margin of safety, especially taken into consideration the additional following concerns: (1) risk of being a state-owned company; (2) FX fluctuations; (3) interest rates eventually reaching a peak; (4) country risk.

Risks and Final Remarks

The main risk of being bearish on BBSEY is that it is a defensive company with very solid fundamentals and, even with its operations under pressure, it still partially offsets its consolidated results based on (1) extraordinarily high margins; (2) good predictability in recurring revenues; (3) historically high payouts; and (4) a strong relationship with Banco do Brasil, which, even facing weaker credit cycles, still guarantees massive distribution of the insurer's products.

However, I am pessimistic in the short to midterm about the persistent challenges in 2Q25 versus the previous quarter, such as (1) a sequential decline in premiums written by Brasilseg, suggesting a loss of commercial traction and potentially competitive pressure; (2) lower-than-expected growth in Brasilprev's reserves; (3) a weaker credit cycle at Banco do Brasil, especially in rural areas, affecting agricultural insurance; (4) valuation not so discounted in the face of escalating risks putting pressure on the safety margin; and finally (5) the relevant exchange rate risk for foreign investors, as any devaluation of the BRL could nullify much of the nominal dividend yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Tariff Win Sends TSMC Higher
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

TSMC climbs to record levels as Taiwan says chip giant exempt from Trump tariffs. (00:23) Samsung (SSLNF) shares rise as Apple taps Texas factory for chip supply. (01:10) United Airlines (UAL) resumes operations after technical glitch. (02:15)

This is an abridged transcript.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) clinched a record high on Thursday after Taiwan said its top chip manufacturer will not have to pay a 100% tariff on such imports to the US.

Taipei-listed TSMC jumped nearly 5% to 1180 won as the time of this recording.

U.S. President Donald Trump said Wednesday he plans to impose a 100% tariff on semiconductor imports with an exemption for companies that manufacture or commit to manufacturing in the U.S.

TSMC has announced significant investments in the U.S recently, including an initial $65 billion to build three plants in Arizona, and an additional $100 billion U.S. investment in March.

Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) climbed on Thursday after Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) said it would source chips from the company's Texas factory.

“Apple is also working with Samsung at its fab in Austin, Texas, to launch an innovative new technology for making chips, which has never been used before anywhere in the world. By bringing this technology to the U.S. first, this facility will supply chips that optimize power and performance of Apple products, including iPhone devices shipped all over the world," the company said in a statement.

If you remember last week SA told you that Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk confirmed that the EV maker has signed a $16.5 billion semiconductor supply deal with Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF).

In a separate development, South Korea’s top trade envoy Yeo Han-koo said on Thursday that Samsung Electronics and SK hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) will not be subject to 100% U.S. tariffs on chips.

Seoul-listed Samsung shares rose as much as 3.2% to 71,000 won.

United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) said that the technical issue that led to over 1,000 flights delayed and dozens canceled across U.S. airports has been resolved.

The airline faced a glitch on Wednesday that affected its computer systems—specifically a problem with the airline's weight and balance computer system.

United Airlines (NASDAQ:UAL) reported the system outage began after 6 p.m. ET. The Federal Aviation Administration halted its departures at several major hub airports, including Chicago, Houston, Denver, Newark, and San Francisco.

According to company statements, the problem was not related to a cyberattack.

The malfunction was resolved later that evening, allowing the airline to resume departures. However, the carrier warned of continued residual delays as staff worked through the backlog and rerouted stranded passengers.

According to Flight Aware, United has canceled 134 flights for today, as of the time of this recording.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

The Week After: How has MSFT performed since FQ4 earnings?

AppLovin outlines 20–30% annual growth target and expands AXON platform globally as e-commerce ramps

IonQ outlines $1.6B cash position and targets 80,000 logical qubits by 2030 while expanding global quantum footprint

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.5% at $64/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.1% at $114,000. Gold is up 0.3% at $3,379.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.2% and the DAX is up 1.7%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: e.l.f. Beauty (NYSE:ELF) -11% – Shares dropped after the cosmetics company reported Q2 results and issued cautious guidance for the first half of the fiscal year.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 10:00 am Atlanta Federal Reserve Bank President Raphael Bostic will participate in a moderated conversation with Ira Birns, president and chief financial officer, World Kinect Corporation, during a virtual fireside chat session with the Florida Institute of CFOs on monetary policy.

  • 10:00 am Wholesale Inventories

  • 3:00 pm Consumer Credit

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: This Is Why Airbnb Can't Outperform The Market Right Now (Earnings Review)
data.description:
Airbnb: Why I Sold a Company I Like

Sometimes we have to invest in a different way than our consumer preferences would suggest. My best example is Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB). I am a fan of the company, I use the app every time I travel, and my wife is a host who manages her parents' seaside house. When the company IPOed, I was there to grab hold of it. I thought the IPO price of around $80 was fair. But the stock skyrocketed, and I could not buy it. I waited and bought when the stock came back from its ATH of $200. My first purchase was around $130, and then I bought back below $100 during the 2022 bear market. I even made it the second-largest bet after Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG) in my publicly visible portfolio on an investing platform where people can copy it. However, I had to acknowledge that, although the company kept improving, its returns were often below the market's. This was mainly because of its demanding valuation, which didn't leave room for true multiple expansion. Although Airbnb's business model displayed strong network effects, a lean capital structure with almost no capex, the strength of holding cash on behalf of guests until stays start, a negative net debt, and solid free cash flow generation, this was not enough to make the stock skyrocket even though the company soon turned to profitability after its IPO and kept growing at a brisk pace. The already stretched valuation (a quarter ago, the fwd 2025 PE was 34 and the fwd 2024 EV/EBITDA was 19) simply didn't allow anything different from happening. This is why, as the stock popped above $160 after earnings, I seized the opportunity and sold, using part of the proceeds to invest in its competitor Booking Holdings (BKNG). After all, BKNG was trading at much more attractive multiples, while continually outpacing market growth, while being the leader in Europe and a rapid scaler in the U.S. The trade has so far been correct, as Airbnb has performed poorly, while BKNG has returned 17%, slightly above the market.

The Broader Picture

While we are used to considering travel as true discretionary spending, I sometimes have the thought that once people earn enough income to pay the bills, one of the first true "extras" that is sought is a nice vacation. This positions travel as the least discretionary among discretionary goods and services. After all, a car is quite expensive, and many can postpone a new purchase if they have to tighten their belts. But these same people could say that, even though it is not the right time to change vehicles, a trip over the weekend or a week around can be moderate expenses (depending on the destination) that can give a sense of pleasure and fulfillment while still being careful of not depleting one's cash reserves.

As of mid-2025, the World Tourism Barometer shows us that tourist arrivals are increasing, while the UN Tourism Confidence Index is reflecting cautious optimism. Over 62% of the world's countries have recovered and surpassed the pre-COVID levels, and we are finally seeing overall international tourist arrivals above the 2019 records.

Real disposable income seems to be rebounding in the U.S. and Europe as inflation moderates. And yet, we hear all over the place that consumers are pulling back on big-ticket items and discretionary spending, even though the Consumer Confidence Index (CCI) has been moving upwards since it bottomed in early April.

With this picture in mind, let's see what Airbnb reported.

Airbnb's Q2 Earnings Review

First of all, Airbnb topped expectations, reporting revenues of $3.1B (consensus of $3.03B) and EPS of $1.03 (consensus: $0.94). The Q3 revenue guidance was expected to be $4.05, and Airbnb released a range between $4.02B and $4.1B. At the midpoint, the difference is around $10M.

Airbnb grew its top line by 13%. The company reached the target of a 34% EBITDA margin; the net income margin of 21% was up 100 bps YoY.

Night and Experiences booked were up 7.4% YoY, gross booking value increased 108% to $23.5B, and gross booking value per night and experiences booked increased 2.9% to $174.48, showing that Airbnb is trying at all costs to keep pricing low.

Free cash flow was almost $100M lower YoY due to higher capex and investments. As a result, we are seeing the TTM FCF moving down to 37% of TTM revenue and being slightly down in absolute value YoY.

Airbnb, too, won't be immune to the new investment cycle wind that is sweeping through most tech companies in the U.S. In particular, it just invested to launch its new segments "Services" and "Experiences" to expand what customers can do on Airbnb to boost engagement and monetize over 1.5B devices that visit the app but don't book.

Overall, the quarter looks pretty solid, as does Airbnb's balance sheet, which holds $11.4B in cash and cash equivalents, as well as $11.1B of funds on behalf of guests.

As of June 30, 2025, we had $11.4 billion of cash and cash equivalents, short-term investments, and restricted cash, as well as $11.1 billion of funds held on behalf of guests. Just by holding this cash, the company generated $226M in quarterly interest income (Q2 2024: $190M). Considering a 20% tax, we have $180M that directly flows into the bottom line, accounting thus for 32.6% of the company's net income. When I think of free money, this is one of the best examples I know. Simply by holding cash on behalf of customers, Airbnb generates a third of its earnings. Airbnb's strong cash position has helped it fund strong share repurchases. In the shareholder letter, Airbnb announced that it has increased its share repurchase authorization up to an additional $6B, which means Airbnb can keep its $1.5B quarterly buyback pace for another year.

Overall, the report was good. However, the stock is down 6.4% in after-hours trading. Why? The main reason can be found in these lines of the report:

As we look ahead to Q3, we’re encouraged by current demand trends. That said, we expect a tougher year-over-year comparison toward the end of the quarter. This dynamic will continue into Q4, putting pressure on growth rates later in the year.

This means that Q3 revenue will be in the range of $4.02 to $4.1B (8%-10% growth) and that we will see stable Q3 nights and seats booked. We should also expect a lower EBITDA margin due to investments, although the FY margin should be above 34%. Since investing is a forward-looking activity, investors are repricing the stock according to this outlook.

Airbnb's True Risk

As a result of what we have seen, we are back to the beginning of the article. In Airbnb's case, there is nothing wrong with the company, apart from its valuation, which offers no room for error or even a slowdown, temporary or permanent, that it may be.

After all, we are talking about a fwd PE of 30.6 and a fwd PEG ratio of 2.7. These multiples can be justified if the company grows at 15%-18%. But Airbnb is growing in the range between 8% and 12%. A good rate, indeed, but not enough to sustain the valuation while leaving a margin for error. We see, in fact, that its valuation Factor Grade is an F, and the growth grade is a D+. These two metrics combine and create the true risk the stock currently faces. I see it as a risk that can put in peril a whole investment, much more than a sudden plunge in travel traffic. This would be a scenario where Airbnb would sooner or later recover, so much is travel ingrained in today's culture. But to heal from being overvalued, there are only two ways: accelerating growth or waiting for earnings to catch up. Airbnb has told us we should not expect the first event to happen this year, so it is even more likely that the stock will keep trailing behind the market this year, as it has done so far.

Therefore, I suggest exiting the position because the opportunity cost of holding Airbnb this year could be high.

data.title: Donville Kent Asset Management July 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Party Like It's 1999?

The Capital Ideas Fund was up +2.05%1 in July, -1.55%1 YTD and up +33.57%1 over the last 12 months.

There has been a lot of media coverage and we’ve been getting questions about the comparisons between the dot com bubble and the current stock market, especially concerning Artificial Intelligence stocks and the overall market valuation. More interesting for us and our investors is the behavior of small caps during and after the dot com bubble, and what that means for the outlook going forward. So, let’s jump back in time.

The year is 2000, the stock market has been ripping higher for years, led by companies tied to the rollout of both the internet and the cellular phone.

The internet is changing the world. Information is at our fingertips with Google, buying online is gaining steam with Amazon and information is in your hand with a Palm Pilot. Surging productivity is boosting profitability and economic output.

The impact on productivity was real. The growth of the economy was real. But human nature has a way of getting ahead of itself. The tech-heavy Nasdaq composite went up almost 600% from 1995 to 2000. What happened next would go down as one of the biggest “bubble bursts” in history.

There is an old adage that bull markets don’t die of old age, they are killed by the Fed. The Federal Reserve raised interest rates six times between June 1999 and May 2000 to combat inflation. The bubble burst in March 2000 and worsened with additional negative events like the Enron bankruptcy in 2001, September 11th tragedy in 2001 and the Worldcom scandal in 2002. Over the next two and half years, the Nasdaq Index would fall close to 80% from the peak.

The internet was revolutionary. How the world operates today versus how business was done pre-internet is almost unrecognizable. But that doesn’t mean you can pay any price for anything related to revolutionary technology.

Fast forward to today. The Artificial Intelligence narrative has many similarities to the Internet narrative, and rightfully so. It is already boosting productivity, improving lives, and generating significant growth and profitability for many companies. BUT, there are some important details to look at.

Let’s use data to cut through the narrative. We have compared the largest 6 tech companies from the 1990s to the 6 largest tech companies today.2 What you’ll notice is that the revenue growth and earnings growth is eerily similar. More importantly, profit margins now are considerably higher, and valuations are high but much lower than 2000. Not cheap, but much lower. This is why we don’t think we’re near a peak like March 2000, plus the fact that interest rates are declining around the globe. The bull murdering Fed isn’t at the doorstep.

Today’s biggest tech stocks are great companies. Higher margin, sitting on a ton of cash, and trading on high valuations, but they are still much cheaper than the dot com peak.

What to worry about is the combination of valuation and disruption.

The AI landscape literally changes everyday. If you follow the daily developments, there are LLMs (large language models) leap frogging to the top of the leaderboard continually. The day over day innovation is astounding. It seems like small, medium, and large companies are in hyperdrive and progressing tech innovation at a heightened pace. The point being, will the leaders of today be the leaders in 5 or 10 years? Paying 38 times earnings does imply a lot of staying power and forward growth, meaning investors are taking on a good amount of valuation risk.

Small vs. Large

Context and history are important, especially with the amount of hyperbole used in the media, but the main point for writing this piece was not to compare the likes of Oracle (ORCL) in 2000 to Amazon (AMZN) today. The catalyst for this topic stems from Morgan Stanley’s July 7th, 2025 report Missing the Forest-and the Trees?3

In our view, the proliferation of new equity investment instruments has encouraged short-termism and weakened investor insight into benchmarks and their diverse, individual components. We believe significant value can be added by investing in differentiated and durable companies. Historically, this quality approach to small-cap investing has delivered attractive risk-adjusted returns, and we believe today’s current valuations point to a compelling, possibly generational, opportunity. – Morgan Stanley, July 7, 2025

Let’s take a look at the first red arrow in the chart above4 and put ourselves in the shoes of the investor in the late 90’s. The sexy internet stocks get all the news headlines and end up being over-owned by investors while the small caps are shunned. Investors get ahead of themselves and chase returns.

We’re seeing the same scenario play out today. The valuations of large caps versus small caps are now back to the extremes last seen in 2000. The narrative being that the value and growth going forward comes from large caps, with a majority of that tied to AI. However, this narrative doesn’t take into account disruption and what type of growth is needed to support theses valuations. Just like the late 1990s and early 2000s, the small caps get shunned and large caps get all the attention. The lesson being what happened after March 2000.

From the peak in March 2000, large-cap tech stocks would decline over 80%, at the same time small caps declined 10% and would go on to return over 100% while large cap tech struggled.5 From the dot com peak in 2000, small caps would be up +107% over the next 7 years while tech stocks returned negative - 46%.5 This isn’t how many investors remember this time in the market and isn’t probably how they’re thinking about the outlook going forward but valuations and disruption matter.

Small companies can’t be bunched together. Some small companies used to be bigger companies and are declining. Some small companies provide niche products and will never grow into big companies. But some small companies are growing quickly and take market share from larger companies.

For example, Nvidia (NVDA), Adobe (ADBE) and AMD (AMD) were small caps in 1999. They proved to be extremely disruptive and have grown into some of the largest companies in the world today. There are many other profitable small caps from the 90s that have performed extremely well, including Copart (CPRT), Waste Connections (WCN), Toro Company (TTC), Fastenal (FAST), and Cintas (CTAS). These companies were $200M, $300M, $1B, $1.5B, and $2B small caps. They are now $45B, $48B, $7B, $54B, and $90B companies. Compared to companies like Cisco (CSCO) and Intel (INTC) that are still worth less today than they were in 2000.

This valuation disconnect is why we like the outlook for the next few years. At the previous lows, like now, all the talk was about the best largest companies. Nothing else mattered. Then small caps would bottom and go into multi-year bull runs. Small caps are unloved now, but you get a lot of downside protection when these stocks are this cheap.

We're obviously investing according to this strategy. We don't own the largest companies in the world as we don't have any significant insight or edge in those markets. We stay focused where we can provide value to our shareholders and provide diversification from the largest ETFs and stocks out there.

The main counterpoint is that most small caps don't have the growth, profitability, or balance sheets of these large companies. However, there are enough smaller companies where you can build a portfolio with more growth, and more profitability at much cheaper prices. That in and of itself is enough to want to diversify there.

Final Thoughts

There are similarities between now and the dot com bubble but also significant differences. With rates falling, companies having higher margins and trading at cheaper valuations, the AI boom still has room to run. As of now, AI investments are accelerating, not declining. The risk being, if the ROI on those AI investments doesn’t pan out, then the current valuations won’t make sense.

That being said, we continue to stick to our expertise in finding up and coming compounders. Currently, there are many high growth, highly profitable, small companies that trade at very attractive valuations. As evidenced by the large vs small valuation difference noted above, we think small companies have both upside from here and protection to the downside based on how cheap they are. Many investors would benefit from diversifying their exposure and taking a lot less valuation risk.

We are encouraged by the strong results we have seen from our investment companies up to this point. Our next monthly commentary will focus on Q2 earnings with most companies reporting in the first few weeks of August. These summaries and reviews will provide some great examples of small caps that are growing faster, with higher profit margins, and trading at much cheaper valuations than large caps.

Sincerely,

Jason & Jesse

info@donvillekent.com
